title: WHEN DOES THE GLOUCESTER TREE REOPEN
summary: Bevan asked when the Gloucester Tree will reopen and what its continued closure is doing to Pemberton tourism operators. Awaiting the Government's reply.
ask: The anticipated reopening date for the Gloucester Tree, what works or approvals remain outstanding, whether partial reopening is possible, and the impact of the closure on Pemberton tourism and local businesses.
answer: Not yet answered. This question was asked on 12 May 2026 and the Government's reply is still outstanding.
finding: The question puts the cost of the Gloucester Tree's continued closure to local operators on the record and awaits the Government's response.

title: WHERE THE PRESCRIBED BURNING MONEY GOES
summary: Bevan asked for a five-year breakdown of prescribed burning and fire management spending, including aviation and the top suppliers. Awaiting the Government's reply.
ask: A five-year breakdown of supplies and services spending on prescribed burning and fire management, the top ten suppliers, aviation costs, and hectares treated by distance from populated places.
answer: Not yet answered. This question was asked on 7 May 2026 and the Government's reply is still outstanding.
finding: The question seeks to open up where the fire-management budget actually goes and awaits the Government's response.

title: SINGLE POWER LINES INTO GROWING TOWNS
summary: Bevan asked why towns including Margaret River, Manjimup and Walpole are served by single transmission lines and what redundancy is planned. Awaiting the Government's reply.
ask: How many outages have hit the Margaret River to Busselton, Beenup to Manjimup and Denmark to Walpole lines over ten years, why some towns rely on a single line, and what network resilience upgrades are planned.
answer: Not yet answered. This question was asked on 6 May 2026 and the Government's reply is still outstanding.
finding: The question puts the supply risk of single-line towns on the record and awaits the Government's response.

title: DOES BOYUP BROOK GET ITS FPC RATES
summary: Bevan asked the Treasurer whether the Forest Products Commission's rates-equivalent payments for land in the Shire of Boyup Brook reach the shire in full. Awaiting the Government's reply.
ask: Whether the Forest Products Commission has paid rates-equivalent amounts for land in the Shire of Boyup Brook, whether that money has been passed to the shire in full, and whether any shortfall against standard rates exists.
answer: Not yet answered. This question was asked on 6 May 2026 and the Government's reply is still outstanding.
finding: The question tests whether a regional shire is being shortchanged on rates for state forestry land and awaits the Government's response.

title: NINETEEN BIOFUEL QUESTIONS, ONE-LINE ANSWER
summary: Across three questions to two ministers, Bevan asked nineteen detailed questions on biofuels from forestry and farm residues; each was answered with a single sentence that a strategy is being developed.
ask: Whether the State has a biofuels strategy, what assessment had been done of fuels from forestry and agricultural residues, and what funding, grants or Commonwealth opportunities had been pursued.
answer: To all nineteen questions, repeated across two further questions to the Minister for Agriculture and Food, the Government said only that, as announced at the 2025 election, it is currently developing a biofuels strategy.
finding: Nineteen specific questions on a regional industry opportunity were met with a single sentence and no detail.

title: THREE LICENCES IN A $40M SANDALWOOD INDUSTRY
summary: Bevan asked what funds and structures support the transition to an Aboriginal-led sandalwood industry; the Government confirmed $280,000 over three years, a dissolved advisory group and only three licences.
ask: What funding supports the transition to an Aboriginal-led sandalwood industry, which advisory body guides wild harvest, and why only three harvesters are licensed despite a doubled quota.
answer: The Government said it invested $280,000 over three years through a capability program, that the WA Sandalwood Advisory Group was dissolved in 2024 having fulfilled its functions, and that three regulation-67 licences are currently in place. It reported about $665,000 spent on compliance and 21.86 tonnes of illegally harvested sandalwood seized between 2021 and 2025.
finding: A $40 million industry meant to transition to Aboriginal leadership rests on $280,000, a dissolved advisory group and three current licences.

title: WESTPORT NOISE THRESHOLDS STILL NOT SET
summary: Bevan asked what underwater noise modelling and limits exist for the Westport works in Cockburn Sound; the Government said modelling is still in progress and thresholds are yet to be set.
ask: Whether baseline underwater acoustic modelling was done before Westport works, whether defined noise limits for pile-driving and dredging exist, and what protects dolphins during breeding periods.
answer: The Government said independent baseline noise studies are published and that construction and operational modelling is in progress, with any acoustic threshold limits to be informed later by the Environmental Protection Authority and Commonwealth agencies.
finding: Noise limits to protect dolphins are described as something to be set later, not figures that exist now.

title: WESTPORT DOLPHIN ASSESSMENT ‘IN PROGRESS’
summary: Bevan asked what baseline data exists on Cockburn Sound's dolphins and how the precautionary principle was applied; the Government said the assessment is still in progress.
ask: Whether the Sound is recognised as dolphin breeding habitat, what baseline population data exists, whether behavioural displacement from noise was assessed, and how the precautionary principle was applied.
answer: The Government said the State and Commonwealth environmental impact assessment processes for the future container port at Kwinana are in progress.
finding: Specific questions on dolphin habitat, baseline data and the precautionary principle were answered only by stating the assessment is ongoing.

title: WESTPORT OVERSIGHT AND REMEDIATION TO COME
summary: Bevan asked what independent oversight and remediation provisions exist for irreversible harm at Westport; the Government described panels and assessment processes still being settled.
ask: What independent scientific panels review Westport's environmental risk, whether all modelling will be public before approval, and what remediation exists if dolphin populations decline after construction.
answer: The Government pointed to the Western Australian Marine Studies Institute Westport Marine Science Program and specialist panels, public environmental review processes, enforceable approval conditions, and remediation provisions to be set through State and Commonwealth approvals.
finding: Oversight structures exist, but the accountability for irreversible harm and the remediation provisions are still to be determined through the approvals process.

title: PENGUIN ISLAND CENTRE CLOSED AND DEMOLISHED
summary: Bevan asked when the Penguin Island Discovery Centre was closed and demolished and how $3.3 million was spent; the Government gave the dates and confirmed the centre will not be rebuilt on the island.
ask: When the Discovery Centre was closed and demolished, how the roughly $3 million allocated for it was spent, and what the current little penguin population is.
answer: The Government said closure was announced in August 2022, the decision not to rebuild on the island and to demolish was announced on 26 August 2022 with works from February 2024, and that of $3.3 million originally allocated, funding was reallocated to demolition, habitat rehabilitation and a mainland feasibility study. It put the 2024 penguin population at 74 to 128 individuals.
finding: The island lost both its visitor centre and any rebuild, while the penguin population it was built around now sits in the low hundreds at most.

title: FERRY ACCESS DREDGED, NO IMPACT ASSESSED
summary: Bevan asked about sand build-up affecting ferry access to Penguin Island and the cost to the operator; the Government gave dredging costs but had not assessed the operator's losses.
ask: What works addressed sand build-up affecting ferry access, how many days of service disruption occurred, and whether the financial impact on the operator had been assessed.
answer: The Government said DBCA removed sand around the jetty in 2024 and 2025 at a cost of $142,821 and $37,505, that there are no formal navigation channels and so no access assessment, and that it does not record when the operator chooses not to run the ferry. It had not assessed the operator's financial impact.
finding: The Government funded dredging but did not assess what restricted access has cost the commercial operator who depends on it.

title: TIGHTER ACCESS RULES, IMPACT NOT MEASURED
summary: Bevan asked what visitor restrictions had been added at Penguin Island since 2016 and their cumulative impact on the tour operator; the Government listed the changes but had not measured the commercial effect.
ask: What seasonal closures and conservation restrictions had been added since 2016, what consultation occurred with the operator, and whether the cumulative commercial impact had been assessed.
answer: The Government listed changes including a four-week extension to the annual breeding-season closure, hot-weather closures above 35 degrees, speed restrictions and habitat works, and said it consults closely with the operator. It said the operator makes its own commercial decisions, without providing an impact assessment.
finding: Access has been progressively tightened since 2016, but the cumulative effect on the licensed tourism operator has not been measured.

title: NO PLAN IF THE TOUR OPERATOR GOES UNDER
summary: Bevan asked whether the Government had assessed the regional economic impact if Perth Wildlife Encounters ceased; the Government said it does not assess individual operators.
ask: Whether the regional economic impact of the operator ceasing had been assessed, the employment it supports, and whether any contingency planning had been done for operator insolvency.
answer: The Government said Tourism WA does not assess the economic contribution of individual operators, but noted it engages through DBCA stakeholder meetings and has assisted the operator's efforts toward sustainability.
finding: The Government has no assessment of what the region loses if the operator fails, and no contingency plan for that outcome.

title: NORTHCLIFFE WATER CARTED IN AT $569 A DAY
summary: Bevan asked what it costs to keep carting water into Northcliffe; the Government confirmed the town still has no permanent supply, with trucked-in water running at $569 a day.
ask: How many kilolitres of water are carted to Northcliffe each day, what it costs, and the total spent over the years the town has gone without a permanent supply.
answer: The Government said about 41 kilolitres a day in November 2025 at $569 per day, with a cumulative cost of $3,095,740 since 2010-11, and noted the arrangement began under the previous Liberal-National Government.
finding: Northcliffe has relied on trucked-in water for more than a decade at over $3 million, and the answer confirmed the cost but offered no permanent supply.

title: PERTH WATCH HOUSE OVER CAPACITY ON 95 DAYS
summary: Bevan asked about custodial staffing and watch house overcrowding; the Government confirmed the Perth Watch House exceeded its design capacity on 95 days, peaking at 131 detainees on Boxing Day.
ask: How custodial and watch house staffing had changed since January 2025, how Protective Service Officers are being used in lock-ups, and how often the Perth Watch House had exceeded its capacity.
answer: The Government said staffing had "increased" without giving a figure, that Protective Service Officers are deployed as operationally required, and that between January 2025 and January 2026 the Perth Watch House exceeded its design capacity of 72 on 95 days, reaching a peak of 131 detainees on Boxing Day.
finding: A facility built for 72 held up to 131 people and was over capacity on 95 days, while the staffing question was answered only with the word "increased."

title: $8.9M ON REGIONAL COURT SECURITY, NO ANALYSIS
summary: Bevan asked what regional court security contractors cost and whether a cost-benefit analysis against auxiliary police had been done; the Government gave the figure and confirmed no analysis exists.
ask: The cost of contracted regional court security in the past year, whether any cost-benefit analysis against using auxiliary police had been done, and what regional justice programs had been reduced.
answer: The Government said contracted court security cost $8,942,063 from November 2024 to October 2025, confirmed no cost-benefit analysis had been undertaken, and noted an Aboriginal-led family violence court in Broome had been delayed by the existing court building.
finding: Nearly $9 million a year is spent on contractors with no analysis of whether auxiliary police would be cheaper, while a regional family violence court is delayed.

title: $29M DROUGHT HIT TO THE PLANTATION ESTATE
summary: Bevan asked why softwood plantations were so vulnerable to drought and why thinning performance is no longer reported; the Government confirmed a $29 million valuation hit and that it will not reinstate the thinning measure.
ask: Why the State's softwood plantations were so vulnerable to drought, how much of the asset value was lost, and why the thinning timeliness measure was dropped from the annual report.
answer: The Government said about 1,500 hectares in the South-West and 6,000 in the Mid-West were significantly affected, with a roughly $29 million negative impact on plantation value, that the thinning indicators were updated under the Government Trading Enterprises Act, and that it would not reinstate the thinning timeliness measure.
finding: The Government confirmed a $29 million drought hit to the estate and declined to restore the public measure that tracks whether plantations are thinned on time.

title: ERADICATION OF SHOT-HOLE BORER ABANDONED
summary: Bevan asked for the advice behind giving up on eradicating the polyphagous shot-hole borer and what it has cost; the Government confirmed eradication was deemed not feasible after almost $60 million spent.
ask: Whether the expert advice to abandon eradication of the polyphagous shot-hole borer would be tabled, how much had been spent each year, and whether the infestation can be contained.
answer: The Government said the National Management Group decided eradication was no longer technically feasible, reported national cost-sharing expenditure rising to about $25 million in 2024-25, and confirmed 1,628 sites identified with 1,522 treated. It said the pest cannot be contained long-term.
finding: After almost $60 million across the response, eradication has been abandoned and the program now aims only to slow a pest that cannot be contained.

title: NO AUDITS ON BUILDERS’ SUPPORT LOANS
summary: Bevan asked whether builders who received state support loans had been audited and how long building disputes take; the Government said no audits had been done and key data is not collected.
ask: Whether builders that received loans under the Builders' Support Facility had been audited, how long building disputes take to resolve, and how many homes have been under construction for more than four years.
answer: The Government said no compliance audits had been undertaken, that building disputes averaged 177 days to resolve in 2024-25 with a review of contract laws due in late 2026, and that data on builds running longer than four years "is not collected."
finding: Public loans to builders have gone unaudited, and the Government does not collect the data needed to see how many families are stuck in years-long builds.

title: DENMARK STUDENTS LEARN ARTS IN OLD HOUSING
summary: Bevan asked whether Denmark Senior High School's music and drama rooms, set up in repurposed houses, meet arts-learning standards; the Government confirmed no full assessment was done.
ask: Whether the repurposed residential buildings used as the school's music and drama classrooms had been assessed against the minimum design standards for arts learning, and whether the school was in any capital works plan.
answer: The Government said the rooms were inspected in 2022 but "a full detailed assessment for new facilities was not undertaken," listed mould remediation works including sub-floor ventilation, drainage and dehumidifiers, and declined the capital works question under Standing Order 77 and Cabinet confidentiality.
finding: The rooms were never assessed against the standard for the subjects taught in them, and the Government is patching moisture problems rather than replacing the buildings.

title: SMALL MILLS COMPETE WHILE BIG FIRMS NEGOTIATE
summary: Bevan asked for the first native-forest timber production data under the new plan and how logs are allocated; the Government detailed contracts and confirmed large firms can negotiate private treaty.
ask: How much native forest timber has been committed under the new Forest Management Plan, whether large firms negotiate private-treaty contracts while small businesses compete, and whether the best commercial price is being achieved.
answer: The Government reported 49 contracts and committed volumes by grade, said all businesses had been offered private-treaty contracts or restricted tenders, and confirmed Simcoa took 79 per cent of mine-site clearing wood. It said no independent value-for-money analysis was done.
finding: The Government confirmed it sells timber by private treaty without independent analysis that the public is getting best value.

title: TIMBER LOSES ITS ENVIRONMENTAL CERTIFICATION
summary: Bevan asked which forest products remain environmentally certified; the Government confirmed mine-site, eucalypt-plantation and hardwood-plantation timber are not certified.
ask: Which forest products sold by the Forest Products Commission remain independently certified, which are not, and whether the Commission would recommence certification to give suppliers and the public assurance.
answer: The Government said softwood, sandalwood and ecological thinning products are certified to AS 4708, but timber from mine-site clearing and eucalypt sharefarm plantations is not, and that hardwood plantation timber is uncertified because there has not been sufficient market demand to justify the cost.
finding: Timber that was once certified as sustainably managed now reaches buyers, including those who require certification, without it.

title: SAWLOGS FROM MINE SITES FALL SHORT OF MILLS
summary: Bevan asked how high-value logs from mine-site clearing are recovered and supplied to sawmills; the Government confirmed supply sits below what mills request.
ask: What volume of high-value logs is recovered from mine-site clearing, whether all are supplied to sawmills for value adding, and whether current supply keeps the sawmill sector viable.
answer: The Government reported 235,000 tonnes of forest products sold from mine-site clearing, including about 6,000 tonnes of high-value recovery logs, said recovery is done by the mine operator with the Commission marketing the logs, and confirmed contracted supply is below the upper limit mills request.
finding: The high-value log supply that mills depend on is recovered by mining companies and falls short of what sawmills ask for.

title: WHO WAS CONSULTED ON THE ART AND SURROGACY BILL
summary: Bevan asked which women's advocacy groups were consulted on the ART and Surrogacy Bill and whether independent women's-rights advocates sat on the expert panel; the answer described general consultation without naming them.
ask: Which women's advocacy groups or independent women's-rights experts were invited to consult on the Assisted Reproductive Technology and Surrogacy Bill, and why independent women's-rights advocates were not included on the Ministerial Expert Panel.
answer: The Government said the Bill rests on the 2018 Sonia Allan Review and a 2022 expert panel review, both open to all bodies including women's interest groups, and that panel members completed conflict-of-interest forms. It did not name specific women's-rights groups or address why independent women's-rights advocates were not on the panel.
finding: Asked who specifically was consulted, the Government pointed to broad review processes and left the named-group and panel-composition questions unanswered.

title: SIX PER CENT OF THE PLANTATION TARGET MET
summary: Bevan put it to the Government that after three years the $350 million plantation program had reached only 6 per cent of the 35,000 hectares promised; the answer did not confirm the original target would be met.
ask: Whether the Government will achieve the 35,000 hectare plantation target originally promised, why the Forest Products Commission has been so slow, and whether progress will be reviewed publicly.
answer: The Government reframed the program as a record $350 million over ten years, said about 20,000 to 25,000 hectares is likely if focused on freehold land, and noted 1,538 hectares established through joint ventures. It did not commit to the original 35,000 hectare figure.
finding: Confronted with 6 per cent delivery against the promised area, the Government lowered the expectation rather than confirming the original target.

title: ELECTION PROMISES MISSING FROM THE BUDGET PAPERS
summary: Bevan asked where six named election promises sit in the budget papers; several were not located and one was confirmed as not funded.
ask: Where the Denmark Primary School car park, the Manjimup Rea Park redevelopment, the Margaret River fire station, regional childcare support and the Boyup Brook Recreation Centre are funded in the budget, given none appears as a specific line item.
answer: The Government confirmed $5.1 million spent on the Margaret River fire station and $5.1 million for regional childcare, said the Boyup Brook Recreation Centre is not funded, and left several of the other named promises without a budget location.
finding: Asked to point to the line items for promised projects, the Government could account for some, confirmed one was not funded, and did not locate the rest.

title: NO BUDGET LINE FOR THE THREE HOSPITAL ASKS
summary: Bevan asked where the budget funds Margaret River Hospital works, a Manjimup CT scanner and the Bridgetown reroof; the answer listed other projects and named none of the three.
ask: Whether the budget includes works for Margaret River Hospital, funding for a Manjimup CT scanner, and the $1 million Bridgetown Hospital emergency department reroof, and the assumptions behind year-on-year cuts to ambulance and patient transport.
answer: The Government listed regional health investments including $471 million for the Bunbury Hospital redevelopment and a virtual emergency department expansion, said urgent care clinics are a federal initiative, and stated ambulance funding would be maintained over the forward estimates. It did not identify funding for any of the three Warren-Blackwood items asked about.
finding: Three specific local hospital asks were answered with a list of other projects, none of which were the ones in the question.

title: MARGARET RIVER HIGH STAGE 2 LEFT TO CABINET
summary: Bevan asked whether the budget funds Margaret River Senior High stage 2 and three other schools; the stage 2 answer cited Cabinet confidentiality.
ask: Whether the budget funds stage 2 of the Margaret River Senior High School expansion, the $12 million Cowaramup classroom block, and maintenance at Bridgetown and Boyup Brook schools.
answer: On stage 2, the Government said it is inappropriate to comment on Cabinet deliberations. On Cowaramup it said funding progresses staged delivery with further funding sought in future budgets. It confirmed a $20,000 sports shed for Bridgetown and no funding for Boyup Brook District High School.
finding: The flagship ask, stage 2 of an overcrowded high school, was deflected to Cabinet confidentiality, and Boyup Brook received nothing.

title: ‘UNCLEAR WHAT FUNDING’ FOR OVERTAKING LANES
summary: Bevan asked whether the budget funds overtaking lanes between Manjimup and Bridgetown; the Minister said it was unclear what funding he meant.
ask: Whether the budget funds the implementation of overtaking lanes between Manjimup and Bridgetown, where it sits and what it includes.
answer: The Government replied that "it is unclear what funding the Member is referring to."
finding: A direct question about overtaking lanes was returned as a question, with no figure and no location in the budget.

title: 7.32 RANGER FTE FOR LEEUWIN-NATURALISTE
summary: Bevan asked how many Aboriginal Ranger Program FTEs work in Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park; the Government gave the funded figure but noted FTEs are not allocated per park.
ask: Whether the Aboriginal Ranger Program funds rangers working within Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park, and if so how many FTEs and on what timeline.
answer: The Government said the program is tenure-blind and funds organisations per FTE rather than per park. It funds the Undalup Association for 7.32 FTE to work across several areas including Leeuwin-Naturaliste National Park.
finding: The park is covered by a single funded organisation whose 7.32 FTE are shared across multiple sites, not dedicated to it.

title: AGED CARE ASSESSMENT FUNDING DROPS TO ZERO
summary: Bevan asked why the aged care assessment line fell to zero and why palliative care funding was discontinued; the Government described short-term Commonwealth contracts ending in 2026.
ask: Why the Aged Care Assessment allocation dropped from $10.2 million to zero across the forward estimates, and why Comprehensive Palliative Care in Aged Care funding was discontinued.
answer: The Government said a 12-month state and Commonwealth contract continues aged care assessments, to be reflected in the mid-year review, and that two palliative care projects continue under a Commonwealth partnership only until 30 June 2026, with no commitment beyond that date.
finding: Both services rest on short-term contracts running to 2026, with no funding shown beyond, in budget lines that read as zero.

title: FIVE STAYING-IN-PLACE HUBS IN WARREN-BLACKWOOD
summary: Bevan asked whether Warren-Blackwood is included in the Staying in Place program; the Government confirmed five existing hubs and two more approved.
ask: Which areas benefit from the Assisting Staying in Place to Thrive program, whether Warren-Blackwood is included, and what data underpins the limited budget allocation.
answer: The Government said there are five existing Staying in Place hubs in the electorate assisted by the initiative, with two further hubs approved for start-up support, and that $250,000 in state funding was approved following a request from Linkwest to expand the program.
finding: The program reaches the electorate through seven hubs, funded by a one-off $250,000 expansion rather than ongoing support.

title: THINNING COSTS EXPECTED TO EXCEED REVENUE
summary: Bevan asked for a forensic breakdown of the forestry budget; the Government confirmed costs of the ecological thinning program are expected to exceed revenue.
ask: For the 2025-26 revenue breakdown by operation, the cumulative shortfall on ecological thinning, and how much land the $350 million softwood fund will ultimately buy.
answer: The Government gave 2025-26 timber revenue as plantations $88.1 million (79 per cent), native forest $7.2 million (6 per cent) and sandalwood $16.2 million (15 per cent), said costs of ecological thinning are anticipated to exceed revenues by more than $5 million, and estimated the softwood fund would secure about 20,000 to 25,000 hectares of freehold land.
finding: The Government confirmed the thinning program runs at a loss and that the $350 million plantation fund will fall well short of the area once promised.

title: WHO PROFITS FROM THE GNANGARA CARBON CREDITS
summary: Bevan asked why the State is buying land for carbon offsets while a private entity replants Gnangara for its own carbon benefit, and what the reservation costs the State; most of it was redirected to the Forestry Minister.
ask: Why the State is purchasing land for carbon offsetting while a private entity replants Gnangara for its own carbon credits, whether the Forest Products Commission is compensating Wesbeam for the reservation, and the total cost to the State.
answer: The Government said no commercial terms had been agreed on the Carbon for Conservation proposal and that DBCA is considering carbon farming on the conservation estate. The questions on compensation and total cost were referred to the Minister for Forestry.
finding: The core questions on who profits from the carbon credits and what the reservation costs the public were passed to another minister rather than answered.

title: 13,000 HECTARES OF GNANGARA STILL UNREHABILITATED
summary: Bevan asked what rehabilitation had been done at Gnangara since pine removal began; the Government confirmed about 13,000 hectares remain unrehabilitated.
ask: What rehabilitation DBCA had undertaken at Gnangara since pine removal began in 2004, how many hectares remain unrehabilitated, and why the pines were reserved rather than re-established for production.
answer: The Government said about 5,000 hectares of native vegetation had been planted since 2004 while roughly 13,000 hectares across Yanchep, Gnangara and Pinjar remain unrehabilitated, with the remaining pines now managed to maximise cone production as a food source for Carnaby's cockatoo.
finding: More than two decades after pine removal began, about 13,000 hectares remain unrehabilitated, with management now driven by cockatoo food rather than timber.

title: 165,000 CUBIC METRES OF TIMBER LOCKED UP
summary: Bevan asked what timber volume was lost when Gnangara was reserved and how Wesbeam's supply is being maintained; the Government detailed the standing volume and a private-treaty karri arrangement.
ask: What volume of timber was lost to the Gnangara reservation and its value, whether the Forest Products Commission can meet its supply contracts to Wesbeam, and on what basis Wesbeam receives karri logs.
answer: The Government said about 165,000 cubic metres of LVL log and 24,000 cubic metres of industrial wood were standing at the time of the cessation, with "no market value as it is not available to harvest." It said karri supply to Wesbeam was tendered to 2023 and has since been by private treaty at the equivalent pine price, with tonnages declining year on year.
finding: A large standing timber resource was reserved, and the shortfall is being covered in part by karri sold to Wesbeam on a private-treaty basis rather than competitive tender.

title: ECOLOGICAL THINNING LOGS SOLD BY PRIVATE TREATY
summary: Bevan asked how ecological thinning is costed and how its logs are sold; the Government confirmed most contracts were privately negotiated and named the companies.
ask: Whether the ecological thinning prescription was published, what the program costs per hectare, whether contracts were tendered, and how stockpiled logs are sold.
answer: The Government put jarrah thinning at about $10,500 per hectare and karri at about $13,500, said contractors were not selected by public tender, and confirmed that aside from tendered firewood and sawlog, all other thinning timber sales were privately negotiated. It listed the businesses awarded private-treaty contracts and reported about 62,000 tonnes held in timber reserves.
finding: A publicly funded thinning program is selling most of its timber through privately negotiated contracts rather than open tender.

title: $350M PLANTATION PLAN, NO REGIONAL ALLOCATION
summary: Bevan asked how much of the $350 million softwood plantation expansion has reached Warren-Blackwood; the Government said it does not allocate by region.
ask: How much of the $350 million committed to expanding softwood plantations has been allocated to Warren-Blackwood, what consultation occurred with local government, and how many hectares have been planted.
answer: The Government said there are no specific allocations by region, with sites chosen on rainfall, soil, distance to market and price. It listed plantation established in the region from January 2022 to May 2025: Boyup Brook 1,505 hectares, Manjimup 402, Nannup 151, Bridgetown-Greenbushes 55 and Donnybrook-Balingup 0.
finding: After three years of a $350 million program, the region had about 2,113 hectares planted, and the Government does not track the spend by region at all.

title: NO DATA ON VOLUNTEER-ONLY AMBULANCE CALLOUTS
summary: Bevan asked how many ambulance callouts in Warren-Blackwood are run by volunteers alone, and the Government confirmed it does not collect that data.
ask: How many paid paramedics cover Warren-Blackwood, where they are based, and how many callouts over three years were handled by volunteers without paid paramedic support.
answer: St John Ambulance is contracted for 41 paid and 8 community paramedics across the South-West, with bases including Manjimup and Denmark. On the volunteer-only callout numbers, the data "is not readily available to WA Health."
finding: The Government could account for its paid paramedics but could not say how often the electorate relies on volunteers alone, because it does not track it.

title: NO ANSWER ON OVERTAKING LANES OR CRASH DATA
summary: Bevan asked for a safety audit, crash figures and the criteria for an overtaking lane on the South Western Highway between Bridgetown and Manjimup; the answer addressed none of them directly.
ask: Whether a safety audit had been done on the Bridgetown to Manjimup section, how many serious or fatal crashes had occurred there in five years, and what criteria an overtaking lane must meet.
answer: The Government listed upgrades made elsewhere on the 244km Bunbury to Walpole route and noted two passing lanes planned at Yornup, but did not provide the crash figures, the criteria, or any commitment for the specific section asked about.
finding: The question named a specific stretch of road and asked for its crash record; the answer pointed to works on other sections and left the safety case for that stretch unanswered.

title: NO COUNT OF REGIONAL ELDERS MOVED TO THE CITY
summary: Bevan asked how many regional residents had been forced into metropolitan aged care for lack of a local bed; the Government said it does not track it.
ask: How many aged care residents from regional areas, including Warren-Blackwood, had been transferred to metropolitan facilities in 12 months for lack of local availability, and whether the Government tracks families forced to seek care outside their communities.
answer: The Government said the Commonwealth funds and regulates aged care and holds the bed data. It reported 99 state co-funded Transition Care places at 96 per cent occupancy and a $100 million low-interest loan scheme for 500 concessional beds, but gave no figure for regional residents relocated.
finding: Asked how many regional elders are being sent away from family for a bed, the Government pointed to the Commonwealth and did not provide a number.

title: NO BUSINESS CASE FOR A MANJIMUP CT SCANNER
summary: Bevan asked whether a CT scanner is planned for Manjimup Hospital and whether the business cases for regional imaging would be released; the answer pointed patients to Busselton.
ask: Whether any planning or funding is underway for a CT scanner at Manjimup Hospital, and whether the Minister would release any business case for diagnostic imaging at Manjimup, Margaret River and Bridgetown hospitals.
answer: The Government said WACHS continues to monitor service needs and that Manjimup patients can access the 128-slice CT scanner at Busselton Health Campus. It did not commit to a scanner, release any business case, or address the travel burden on elderly patients.
finding: The remedy offered for the absence of local imaging was a hospital hours away, and the requested business cases were not released.

title: NO LOCAL AGED CARE BED OR WAITLIST DATA
summary: Bevan asked for a list of aged care facilities, occupancy and waitlists across Warren-Blackwood; the Government said the Commonwealth holds the data.
ask: For a list of aged care facilities in Warren-Blackwood with bed numbers, occupancy and waitlists, the current average wait for a residential bed, and any planned upgrades for 2025-26.
answer: The Government said the Commonwealth Department of Health, Disability and Ageing is responsible for aged care access and holds the data on beds.
finding: A direct request for the region's bed numbers, wait times and planned upgrades was answered only by naming who holds the data.

title: NO COUNT OF REGIONAL AGED CARE WORKERS
summary: Bevan asked how many aged care workers serve Warren-Blackwood and what is done to keep them; the Government pointed to the Commonwealth and listed fee-free courses.
ask: The total number of aged care workers across the electorate, how many are qualified nurses or allied health, and what incentives exist to attract and retain them in rural areas.
answer: The Government said the Commonwealth holds the relevant workforce data, and noted it has made several qualifications fee-free, including Certificate III in Individual Support and Certificate IV in Ageing Support.
finding: The workforce numbers were not provided; the retention answer was a list of training courses rather than a count of who is actually on the ground.

title: NO REPORT ON RURAL AGEING-IN-PLACE GAPS
summary: Bevan asked how many older residents receive home support, whether non-volunteer medical transport exists, and whether the Government had assessed rural service gaps; no report was tabled.
ask: How many older Warren-Blackwood residents receive Commonwealth home support or Home Care Packages, whether any non-volunteer medical transport is provided in towns without hospitals, and whether the Government had assessed transport and service gaps for ageing in place.
answer: The Government again said the Commonwealth funds and regulates aged care, noted transport is a category within Commonwealth home support, and pointed to the Regional Pensioner Travel Card of up to $775 a year. No assessment report was tabled.
finding: The question about whether rural ageing-in-place gaps had been assessed was met with a pensioner fuel subsidy and no report.

title: 219 WORKERS ON FORESTRY TRANSITION SUPPORT
summary: Bevan asked what the forestry transition has delivered for Warren-Blackwood; the Government gave figures for workers supported and grants paid.
ask: How many former forestry workers in Warren-Blackwood had received transition support, what retraining was offered, what regional funding was allocated, and whether any local workers or businesses had been unsuccessful.
answer: The Government said 219 workers had been supported, with up to $8,000 each for reskilling, over 50 regional outreach sessions, and $17.69 million in business grants in Warren-Blackwood under the $80 million Native Forest Transition fund. It confirmed some grant applicants were unsuccessful under the competitive streams.
finding: The Government confirmed the headline figures for the transition, and that not every local business that applied was funded.
